name: Camino Francés: Roncesvalles Loop
name_original: Bizkarreta, Ibañeta, Auritz, Roncesvalles – Pilgrim's Route – Ruta Circular
description: This difficult 19.6-mile (31.5 km) touring cycling route explores the Pyrenees and Camino de Santiago near Roncesvalles.
region_intro: Hiking around Erro offers access to the diverse landscapes of Spain's Navarre region, nestled within the Pyrenees mountains. The area features lush forests, rolling hills, and several valleys, providing varied terrain for outdoor activities. Rivers like the Arga also contribute to the scenic routes available. This region is characterized by significant elevation changes, from valley floors to mountain passes. Best hiking trails around Erro The most popular hiking route is Cima del Adi from Collado de Urkiaga (GR 11 & GR 12), a 4.9 miles (7.8 km) trail that takes 3 hours 22 minutes to complete. This route ascends to Cima del Adi, offering views of the Pyrenees. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cascada Irurrekaeta from Camping Espinal, an easy 4.0 miles (6.4 km) path. It leads to Cascada Irurrekaeta through forested paths. Local hikers also love the Mendiaundi – circular from Refugio Sorogain, a 5.7 miles (9.2 km) trail leading through the Mendiaundi area, often completed in about 3 hours 20 minutes. Hiking around Erro is defined by Pyrenees mountains, lush forests, and rolling hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to challenging treks. The routes in Erro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 140 reviews. More than 1200 hikers have used komoot to explore Erro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when hiking around Erro?
A: Hiking around Erro offers a diverse range of landscapes. You'll find yourself amidst the majestic Pyrenees mountains, traversing lush forests, and navigating rolling hills. The region also features several valleys, such as the Ordesa Valley and Aguas Tuertas Valley, providing varied and scenic terrain.

Q: How many hiking trails are available in the Erro area?
A: There are over 45 hiking routes available around Erro, catering to various preferences and skill levels. This includes a mix of easy strolls, moderate treks, and more challenging paths through the Pyrenees.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families in Erro?
A: Yes, Erro offers several easy hiking trails. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970216676/cascada-irurrekaeta-from-camping-espinal" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cascada Irurrekaeta from Camping Espinal&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.0-mile path that leads through forested areas to a waterfall, making it a great option for a relaxed outing.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks to look out for on Erro's trails?
A: Erro's trails are rich with natural beauty and historical landmarks. You can explore areas with crystal-clear waters like the Lakes of Irati, or discover scenic rivers such as the Arga. Historic sites include the 12th-century Romanesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1014822" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roland Fountain&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/717452" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibañeta Pass&lt;/a&gt; offers breathtaking panoramic views of the Pyrenees.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Erro?
A: Yes, circular routes are available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e969989714/mendiaundi-circular-from-refugio-sorogain" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mendiaundi – circular from Refugio Sorogain&lt;/a&gt;, a 5.7-mile trail that takes you through the scenic Mendiaundi area, typically completed in about 3 hours 20 minutes.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Erro?
A: The hiking routes in Erro are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 140 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the diverse terrain, from challenging mountain ascents to tranquil forest paths, and the stunning views of the Pyrenees.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Erro?
A: Given its location in the Pyrenees, the best time to hike around Erro is generally from spring through autumn. During these seasons, the weather is more favorable, and the trails are typically clear of snow, allowing for comfortable exploration of the lush forests and mountain passes. Winter hiking can be challenging due to snow and colder conditions.

Q: Are there any trails that offer significant elevation gain for a challenging hike?
A: Erro is situated in a mountainous region, so many trails feature significant elevation changes. For a challenging hike with notable ascent, consider routes that lead to higher points in the Pyrenees. The area has 10 difficult routes, offering substantial climbs and rewarding views.

Q: Can I find trails with waterfalls in the Erro region?
A: Yes, you can find trails that lead to waterfalls. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e970216676/cascada-irurrekaeta-from-camping-espinal" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cascada Irurrekaeta from Camping Espinal&lt;/a&gt; is an example of a route that takes you to a beautiful waterfall through forested paths. The broader Pyrenees region also features iconic waterfalls like the Cola de Caballo in the nearby Ordesa Valley.

Q: Are there any specific mountain passes or viewpoints worth visiting near Erro?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/717452" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibañeta Pass&lt;/a&gt; is a significant viewpoint, offering expansive panoramic views of the surrounding Pyrenees. It's also historically important, commemorating the Battle of Roncesvalles with the Roland Monument. Other passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4817151" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Erro Pass&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/985119" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Artesiaga Pass&lt;/a&gt; also provide stunning vistas.

Q: What are the options for parking when accessing hiking trails in Erro?
A: While specific parking details vary by trailhead, many popular hiking starting points in the Erro region, especially those near villages or refuges like Refugio Sorogain or Camping Espinal, typically offer designated parking areas for hikers. It's advisable to check the specific route details on komoot for parking information related to your chosen trail.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly hiking trails in Erro?
A: Erro's natural landscapes are generally welcoming for dogs, and many trails are suitable for hiking with your canine companion. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with livestock or protected wildlife, and to check local regulations for specific trails or nature reserves you plan to visit.
tips: The monastery of Roncesvalles is one of the first important pilgrimage sites on the Camino Francés after crossing the Pyrenees. Pilgrims can also find accommodation here for the night in a monastic atmosphere - an experience not to be missed on the Camino.

In the Battle of Roncevaux on 15 August 778, a Basque force defeated Charlemagne's army at the Roncevaux Pass, a high mountain pass in the Pyrenees. This happened at the end of Charlemagne's campaign on the Iberian Peninsula. According to the classical version, the Carolingian rearguard was ambushed and the leader Roland was killed. The epic Chanson de Roland is based on this, except that the attackers are changed to Saracens. Historically, it is at most possible that the Basques were reinforced by Muslim troops. Another hypothesis is that the Franks suffered a defeat in a larger, regular battle with the Basques. 
Wikipedia

In the 11th century, the monastery of San Salvador is said to have stood here at the top of the pass, on the foundations of which a small chapel was built in the 1960s. A little further up the hill is the Roland Monument, which commemorates the Battle of Roncesvalles by the Basques against the French army under Charlemagne on August 15, 778. Above all, the panoramic view from up there is really breathtakingly beautiful!
Modern chapel, and a memorial stone in memory of Roeland, the faithful paladin of Charlemagne, who is said to have fallen here.
THE alternative to the bridge with a corresponding adventure factor! This can vary slightly depending on the weather.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging 31.5-kilometer circular route is estimated to take approximately 2 hours and 24 minutes to complete by touring bicycle. However, this duration can vary based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and the specific conditions of the trail.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and a 'hard bike ride,' demanding very good fitness from cyclists. It features a significant elevation gain of almost 490 meters and some segments may be unpaved and difficult, potentially requiring you to push your bike. It's not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route features mostly paved surfaces, offering smooth cycling for much of the way. However, be prepared for some unpaved and difficult segments where you might need to dismount and push your bike. The broader area, including parts of the Camino de Santiago, can include gravel tracks and loose ground.

faqs.question: What historical landmarks and natural features will I see on the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several significant points of interest. Highlights include the Forest Path Near Roncesvalles, the historic Collegiate Church and Monastery of Roncesvalles, and the Roncesvalles Battle Memorial. A key point is the Ibañeta Pass and the Roland Monument, which also features the San Salvador Chapel built on an 11th-century monastery's foundations.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: Expect breathtaking panoramic views of the Pyrenees. The trail winds through scenic roads and forests, offering picturesque landscapes, especially around the Ibañeta Pass. You'll also pass through beautiful beech and oak woods, characteristic of the region.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during the spring (late April to June) and autumn (September to October) when the weather is milder and the scenery is vibrant. Summer can be pleasant but may have hotter temperatures, while winter cycling can be challenging due to snow and colder conditions in the Pyrenees.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access this circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the route is circular and passes through Bizkarreta, Ibañeta, Auritz, and Roncesvalles, Roncesvalles is a common starting point for pilgrims and cyclists. You can typically find parking facilities in or near Roncesvalles, which offers good access to the route. Specific parking details may vary, so checking local information upon arrival is advisable.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route significantly overlaps with sections of the historic Camino Francés - 01 (42% of the route) and the European long distance path E3 - part Spain (42%). It also intersects with smaller portions of trails like Lepoeder (GR12) - Auritz (GR11) and Txangoa - Roncesvalles.

faqs.question: Is the Bizkarreta, Ibañeta, Auritz, Roncesvalles – Pilgrim's Route – Circular Route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While many outdoor trails in the Pyrenees are dog-friendly, this route is primarily designed for touring bicycles and includes sections that are part of the Camino de Santiago. If cycling with a dog, ensure it is well-trained and capable of keeping pace, especially on potentially unpaved or challenging segments. Always keep your dog on a leash and clean up after it.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public circular route. However, if you plan to visit specific attractions like the Collegiate Church in Roncesvalles, there might be separate entry fees for those sites.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through towns like Bizkarreta, Auritz, and Roncesvalles, you will find options for cafes, pubs, and accommodation. Roncesvalles, being a major stop on the Camino de Santiago, offers several pilgrim hostels (albergues) and other lodging choices, as well as places to eat and resupply.
